prevalence.source: Allergy/anaphylaxis epidemiology syntheses; WHO allergy context
prevalence.trend: increasing recognition and emergency presentations (food and drug triggers)

burden.dalyPerCapita: low; high acuity events and anxiety/avoidance burden
burden.source: WHO/IHME injury and acute allergy context

vulnerablePopulations: patients with limited access to emergency services and epinephrine
rural areas with longer EMS response times
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical criteria (acute onset with airway/breathing/circulation compromise or hypotension after allergen exposure); serum tryptase supports diagnosis when measured; response to epinephrine

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no screening; diagnosis and management after event
publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: Millions with lifetime risk; acute events far fewer
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Moderate; depends on timely epinephrine use and access to allergist follow-up

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Emergency diagnosis in UPA/ER; allergy referral for trigger work-up and prevention planning
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Immediate IM epinephrine; airway/oxygen/IV fluids as needed; observation for biphasic reactions; prescribe/educate on epinephrine auto-injector where available; avoidance plan and action plan
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Limited access and cost barriers for epinephrine auto-injectors, uneven allergy specialist availability
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Emergency services can treat acute events; increasing public and professional awareness
